name: Wayne Andre
abstract: Wayne Andre (November 17, 1931 – August 26, 2003) was an American jazz trombonist, best known for his work as a session musician.

interviews.title: Wayne Andre interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 7, 2002

concerts.label: New York Jazz Repertory Company: The Music of Quincy Jones
New York Jazz Repertory Company: The Music of George Russell
Tribute to Charles Mingus: Mingus Music

works.t: Don't Be Afraid, the Clown's Afraid Too
The Shoes of the Fisherman's Wife (Are Some Jive Ass Slippers)

concerts.label: Newport Jazz Festival: Benny Goodman Octet
Kool Jazz Festival: Mel Torme and George Shearing

quotes.text: I did a concert, I’ll never forget this, I did a concert and I had a big solo in the concert. I did a concert with Tony Bennett at Carnegie Hall. And on the first half of the concert was Robert Farnum and we were doing the music from “Porgy & Bess.” And I just have to tell you the story how I ended up getting this solo because Buddy Morrow was actually the first trombone player but this thing, Buddy came across it and Buddy is a wonderful, wonderful cat but this thing for some reason came across written in the alto clef or some damn thing like that. And it was a spooky solo because the whole band stopped and you go, now listen carefully, [scats] you modulate right at the top [scats]. And it modulates right away and it was something that’s beautiful but you kind of had to either hear it or read it. So Buddy says, “Man, why don’t you play this thing?” So that’s how I ended up getting the solo you know. And after the rehearsal I saw all these microphones, Robert Farnum, I didn’t know Robert Farnum at all. I says, “You know this is Carnegie Hall. This music is so beautifully orchestrated. All these microphones are going to ruin the music.” He said, “Oh no, we don’t need any microphones, what are you talking about, microphones?” So because of me, all the microphones, I don’t know if they were taken down but they were shut off. And that concert was a big success concert. I’m not saying I was the reason it was a big success but I’m just saying that I hate music that sounds like over — anyways the North Texas Band, I said I don’t want any micro — we’ll have one microphone solo for a trumpet solo or just maybe two or three solo mics and that’s all we need. And my music that I write and that I played on that concert by and large — I have to be careful I don’t give myself too many accolades, but most of it, each piece is different. Each piece is different and the first piece doesn’t sound like the fourth piece. Like I did this real production number and it came out really terrific, “My Man’s Gone Now” and this was kind of maybe the highlight of the concert. And then we ended up doing the thing I wrote for Doc Severinsen which actually Bill Watrous recorded with Danny Styles, that piece “Ayo,” that kind of ended the concert. But the thing is, I haven’t © Fillius Jazz Archive -18-
quotes.caption: Wayne Andre recalls a Tony Bennett concert at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Wayne Andre interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 7, 2002

about_quotes.speaker: Alan Raph
about_quotes.evidence: Wayne Andre was the slide trombone. Whatever Brookmeyer did I would do it as quick as I could... And Wayne and I would look at each other like oh my God, isn’t this wonderful.
about_quotes.doc_title: Alan Raph interviewed by Monk Rowe, Danbury, Connecticut, November 20, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Randy Brecker
about_quotes.evidence: It was me, Mike and Sanborn and a great trombone player, Wayne Andre. We showed up at the session... So it became our job to write a chart.
about_quotes.doc_title: Randy Brecker interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, December 12, 2022

about_quotes.speaker: Gap Mangione
about_quotes.evidence: Paul Faulise, Tony Studd, Wayne Andre, that’s the three trombones... We actually hired this guy... Great players.
about_quotes.doc_title: Gap Mangione interviewed by Monk Rowe, Rochester, New York, January 11, 2012

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Crow
about_quotes.evidence: I was working with Joe Wilder at that time, and Wayne Andre and Jimmy Maxwell.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bill Crow interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, October 18, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Phil Flanigan
about_quotes.evidence: So my dad’s connected with that upstate jazz scene of which there are others, like Wayne Andre, the trombonist, went to Sampson
about_quotes.doc_title: Phil Flanigan interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Diego, California, February 15, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Quentin Jackson
about_quotes.evidence: Wayne Andre was there. I think Freddie Zito was there, and Bill, he just came in town to do that show.
